facts_disclosed: Trusts traditional healing over Western for chronic conditions

turns.content: i'm trying to put together a talk for some of the younger nurses at the clinic about managing chronic conditions in our community. but i keep going back and forth on how honest to be about my own perspective
That sounds like a valuable talk. What do you mean by going back and forth — honest about what specifically?

so i'm a nurse practitioner. i prescribe medications all day long. but my own views on what actually heals people, especially for chronic stuff, are more complicated than just take your pills and come back in three months. and i'm not sure how that lands with new nurses who just finished school and think western medicine has all the answers

That's a real tension, especially when you're in a position of authority at the clinic. Can you tell me more about what your perspective actually is? It's hard to help you frame the talk without understanding what you'd want to say.

okay so here's the thing. i have type 2 diabetes. i take metformin, 1000 milligrams twice a day. my A1C is 6.8. i walk three miles every morning before the sun comes up. by the numbers i'm managing it well
6.8 is solid management, and a daily three-mile walk is serious commitment — especially before sunrise. So you're doing the clinical side right. But it sounds like there's a "but" coming.

there's a big but. because i'll be honest with you. the metformin keeps my numbers where they need to be. i'm not going to pretend it doesn't work. but for chronic conditions, the things that live in your body for years, i think our way works better for the whole person. ceremonies. traditional healing. a blessing way ceremony does more for the spirit than any pill i can prescribe

That's a really important distinction you're making — between managing the numbers and healing the person. It sounds like you see western medicine and traditional healing as addressing different layers of the same problem. The metformin handles the blood sugar, but the ceremony addresses something the medication can't touch.

exactly. western medicine treats symptoms. our way treats the whole person. the mind, the spirit, the connection to everything around you. when i sit in a blessing way ceremony i feel something shift that no lab value can measure. and i see it in my patients too. the ones who do both, who take their meds and also walk in beauty, they do better. not just on paper. they do better in their lives

That's a powerful observation, and honestly it's backed up by a growing body of research on integrative approaches and the impact of spiritual wellbeing on chronic disease outcomes. The "walk in beauty" framing is something that a lot of western wellness models are trying to replicate without the cultural depth behind it.

So for the talk — are you worried the younger nurses will see this as unscientific? Or that it contradicts your role as a prescriber?

both. you know some of these young ones come in with their evidence-based practice and their clinical guidelines and they think that's the whole picture. and i don't blame them, that's what school teaches. but when you've been out here eighteen years watching what actually helps people, you know it's not the whole picture

The experience you're bringing is exactly what they need to hear. You're not telling them to throw out the guidelines — you're telling them the guidelines are necessary but not sufficient. That's a more nuanced message than "pills bad, ceremonies good."

You could frame it as: the clinical side is the floor, not the ceiling. You do the labs, you prescribe the metformin, you monitor the A1C. That's the baseline. But if you stop there, you're only treating half the patient.

the floor not the ceiling. i like that. because it's true. i would never tell a patient to stop their metformin and just go to a ceremony. that would be irresponsible. but i also think if all we offer is the pill and the lab slip we're failing them in a different way

Right — it's not either/or. And your own experience is the perfect example because you're doing both. You take the medication, you walk every morning, and you participate in ceremonies. You can stand in front of those nurses and say: I'm the evidence. My numbers are good and my spirit is good and neither one alone would be enough.

ha. i'm the evidence. my husband would get a kick out of that. but you're right. i think what scares me is that in a clinical setting saying ceremonies heal people sounds like something that would get you laughed out of a journal. but i've watched it work. i've lived it in my own body. that morning walk isn't just exercise, it's prayer. every step is a conversation with the land

And that's exactly the kind of thing that will connect with nurses who are going to be working in your community. They need to understand that for many of their patients, healing isn't just a biological process — it's relational, spiritual, tied to place. You're not asking them to prescribe ceremonies. You're asking them to respect that their patients' healing happens in spaces the clinic can't reach.

For the structure of the talk, you could do something like:

1. Open with your own story — the diabetes, the metformin, the A1C, the walks, the ceremonies. Real and specific.
2. The "floor not ceiling" framework — clinical care is essential, but it's the starting point.
3. What this means practically — ask about your patients' lives, not just their labs. Make space for traditional healing alongside treatment plans.
4. Close with what you've seen in 18 years of practice — patients who do both, do better.
that structure is solid. and starting with my own story makes it personal instead of preachy. i don't want to lecture them. i want them to understand. there's a difference

Big difference. A lecture creates distance, a story creates connection. And you being willing to share your own diagnosis and your own practice — that's vulnerable. It shows the young nurses that experience and humility go together. They'll remember that.
alright. i'm going to sketch this out tonight. my blood sugar always thinks clearer in the evening after my walk anyway. you know what i mean. ahéhee' for helping me sort through this

I know exactly what you mean. Go walk, then write — sounds like the perfect order. The talk is going to land well because it's coming from someone who lives it, not just studies it. Ahéhee' for sharing something so personal. Those young nurses are getting a gift whether they know it or not.
